text: In this video, we present the case of a seventy-three-year-old woman who presented to our institution with signs and symptoms of acute small bowel obstruction. She has an extensive past medical history, including schizophrenia, for which she lives in a group home. The patient underwent CT imaging. As is visible on the scout image, there is evidence of dilated small bowel loops and perhaps air within the gallbladder, marked by the circle. On axial imaging, there is evidence of large calcified gallstones within the gallbladder as well as periduodenal inflammation with evidence of a fistulus tract from the gallbladder to the duodenum. Further, in the pelvis, there is evidence of a gallstone within the small intestine causing obstruction. These findings are re-demonstrated on the coronal reconstructions. We suspected a cholecystoduodenal fistula as well as a small bowel obstruction from a gallstone within the intestinal tract consistent with gallstone ileus.

Traditionally, gallstone ileus has two treatment goals. The first and most important being removal of the obstructing gallstone within the small intestine. In select patients, an attempt at cholecystectomy with cholecystoduodenal fistula division may be attempted. However, as most patients present moribund, the additional time and added risk may not be warranted. In an effort to utilize the benefits of minimally invasive surgery, these patients may be dealt with in a laparoscopic manner. We present a novel option that, in the setting of patients who have large residual stones within the gallbladder lumen, A laparoscopic cholecystotomy with extraction of these stones and gallbladder closure may be utilized as a safe alternative for cholecystectomy.

The surgeon elevates the greater omentum to expose the small bowel, revealing the area of obstruction caused by the gallstone. Sutures are placed, and a longitudinal enterotomy is performed to retrieve the stone. The enterotomy is then closed transversely to prevent stricturing, and the remainder of the small intestine is examined for additional stones.

The surgeon focuses on the right upper quadrant, where an extensive inflammatory response around the gallbladder is visible. The surrounding omentum is carefully dissected to expose the gallbladder fundus. A cholecystotomy is performed, and gallstones are retrieved from the gallbladder lumen. The cholecystoduodenal fistula is clearly visible, with no residual stones present. The area is irrigated, and the gallbladder is closed with interrupted figure-of-eight silk sutures.
The surgeon irrigates the upper abdomen and places a drain near the gallbladder following the cholecystotomy procedure.
